papers.title: Generative AI-Based Drug Design: Target-Aware Molecular Generation for EGFR Type I Inhibitors with Multiplatform Docking Validation.
papers.abstract: The development of selective kinase inhibitors remains an ongoing challenge in the drug development process, particularly regarding resistance mutations in the epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR). EGFR resistance mutations, particularly T790 M and C797S variants, pose significant challenges in oncology therapeutics, necessitating novel approaches to identify resistance-circumventing inhibitors. In this study, a target-conditioned generation framework is proposed, which incorporates ATP-binding pocket information into structural generation through structure-aware conditioning. A three-layer
papers.summary: This study presents a generative AI framework that incorporates structural information from the ATP-binding pocket to design novel EGFR inhibitors capable of overcoming common resistance mutations (T790M and C797S). The approach uses structure-aware conditioning to generate target-specific molecules, which are then validated through multiplatform docking simulations. This work demonstrates a practical application of generative AI for designing resistance-circumventing kinase inhibitors.

papers.title: DeepMech: a machine learning framework for chemical reaction mechanism prediction.
papers.abstract: The ability to predict the complete, step-by-step mechanism of chemical reactions from first principles remains a grand challenge in science. The importance of chemical reaction mechanisms (CRMs) pervades almost all domains such as prebiotic chemistry, drug discovery, materials science and so on. Uncovering CRMs remains a complex task, traditionally reliant on expert-driven experiments or expensive quantum chemical computations. While deep learning (DL) studies have shown promise in predicting reaction outcomes, they have largely ignored important intermediates and mechanistic steps en route t
papers.summary: DeepMech is a new machine learning framework that can predict the complete step-by-step mechanisms of chemical reactions, including intermediates, rather than just final products. This approach could accelerate discovery in fields like drug development and materials science by replacing expensive quantum chemistry calculations or time-consuming expert analysis.

papers.title: Prioritising search for virtual screening via preliminary interpretable low-feature likelihood-based rankings of drug-target activity measures.
papers.abstract: Current AI-based Virtual Screening (VS) methods seek to manage ultra-large molecular libraries. To this end, they develop increasingly efficient heuristics to rank ligands by their predicted activity against a target protein. However, these methods remain computationally demanding due to the billion-scale compound libraries that must be evaluated without prior, informed guidance.
papers.summary: This paper introduces a method to prioritize which compounds to screen in virtual screening by using a simple, interpretable ranking based on a few key features, reducing the computational burden of evaluating billion-scale libraries. The approach provides a preliminary likelihood-based ranking of drug-target activity before applying more complex AI methods, making the overall screening process more efficient.

papers.title: The role of AI-assisted drug repurposing in neurological disorders: a systematic review of validation strategies, challenges and opportunities.
papers.abstract: Neurological disorders refer to a diverse group of conditions that affect the brain, peripheral nerves, and spinal cord and impair socioemotional, cognitive, motor, and sensory functions. Alzheimer's disease (AD), Multiple Sclerosis (MS), Parkinson's disease (PD), Huntington's disease (HD), and Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) are some of the well-known neurodegenerative diseases that affect millions of people worldwide. Despite the advanced technologies and nano-drug delivery systems, the success rate of developing drugs for neurological disorders is significantly low. Among several constr
papers.summary: This systematic review examines how AI is being used to repurpose existing drugs for neurological disorders like Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, and ALS. It evaluates the validation strategies, challenges, and opportunities in this approach, highlighting that despite advanced technologies, drug development success for these conditions remains low.

papers.title: AI-guided identification of natural CTSL inhibitors with therapeutic potential for renal injury.
papers.abstract: Cathepsin L (CTSL) is a prominent therapeutic target for kidney injury, yet clinically available CTSL inhibitors remain limited. Here, we developed an artificial intelligence (AI)-assisted discovery strategy to identify novel CTSL inhibitors from a natural products library. Through a robust deep learning model and molecular docking, we screened 200 molecules from natural products library for experimental validation. Active candidates were further analyzed by molecular dynamics simulations to characterize binding modes and key CTSL-ligand interaction networks, followed by evaluation of therapeu
papers.summary: Researchers developed an AI-assisted strategy combining deep learning and molecular docking to identify natural compounds that inhibit Cathepsin L (CTSL), a therapeutic target for kidney injury. They screened 200 natural products, validated active candidates experimentally, and used molecular dynamics simulations to characterize binding interactions. This approach identified novel CTSL inhibitors with potential for treating renal injury.

papers.title: Molecular design beyond training data with novel extended objective functionals of generative AI models driven by quantum annealing computer.
papers.abstract: Deep generative modeling to stochastically design small molecules is an emerging technology for accelerating drug discovery and development. However, one major issue in molecular generative models is their lower frequency of drug-like compounds. To solve this problem, we develop a novel, quantum annealing, generative-model approach for optimizing deep generative models, an approach that includes integrating with a D-Wave annealing quantum computer. Of particular note, our neural hash function (NHF) is used simultaneously as a regularization scheme and binarization scheme, of which the latter i
papers.summary: This paper presents a novel approach to molecular design that uses quantum annealing to optimize deep generative models, addressing the common problem of low frequency of drug-like compounds. The key innovation is a neural hash function that serves both as a regularization and binarization scheme, enabling the generation of molecules beyond the training data distribution.

papers.title: The use of artificial intelligence (AI) in neuropsychiatric drug discovery: current challenges and future directions.
papers.abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) has emerged as a powerful tool for solving real world problems across a wide range of industries and is increasingly being utilised by pharmaceutical companies to discover novel drug targets, biomarkers, and new drugs. Several AI-driven small molecules have entered clinical trials over the past few years, but their fate remains unknown. Currently, no commercially available compounds have been developed solely using AI approaches. In this perspective, we examine the current use of AI in drug discovery for neuropsychiatry. The pace of drug discovery in neuropsychiatr
papers.summary: This perspective article examines the current use of artificial intelligence in neuropsychiatric drug discovery, noting that while AI has identified several small molecules now in clinical trials, no commercially available drugs have been developed solely through AI approaches. The authors discuss the unique challenges and future directions for applying AI to discover novel drug targets, biomarkers, and treatments for neuropsychiatric disorders.

papers.title: AlphaFold-based peptide structure prediction: Opportunities, limitations, and future directions.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of peptide structures and peptide-receptor complexes is essential for rational peptide drug development. However, the inherent conformational flexibility of short and disordered peptides presents a fundamental challenge. The AlphaFold model series, which has progressed from AlphaFold2 through AlphaFold-Multimer to AlphaFold3, has substantially advanced computational peptide structure prediction through innovations in geometric reasoning (invariant point attention) and interface-focused confidence metrics (ipTM score), achieving high accuracy for both monomeric peptide struc
papers.summary: This review examines the use of AlphaFold models for predicting peptide structures and peptide-receptor complexes, a key step in peptide drug development. It highlights how AlphaFold2, AlphaFold-Multimer, and AlphaFold3 have improved accuracy through geometric reasoning and confidence metrics, despite the challenge of peptide flexibility. The paper discusses current limitations and future directions for applying these tools in drug discovery.

papers.title: 3Br-MGD: few-shot toxicity prediction with a three-branch deep encoder and meta-learning framework.
papers.abstract: Predicting the toxicity of pharmaceutical compounds remains a major challenge in drug discovery. Early and accurate toxicity assessment is essential for eliminating harmful candidates before costly preclinical and clinical testing, thereby improving patient safety, reducing development costs, and accelerating the drug development process. Despite advances in computational toxicology, existing methods often struggle to capture complex molecular characteristics and maintain robust performance under limited-data conditions. To address these challenges, we propose 3Br-MGD, a novel three-branch fra
papers.summary: This paper introduces 3Br-MGD, a deep learning framework that combines a three-branch encoder with meta-learning to predict drug toxicity from limited data. The method outperforms existing approaches by better capturing complex molecular features, addressing a key bottleneck in early-stage drug discovery.

papers.title: AD-LSF: A Multimodal Framework for Drug-Target Interaction Prediction Based on Asymmetric Dynamic Gated Modulation and Adaptive Latent Signal Fusion.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of drug-target interactions (DTIs) is fundamental to drug discovery and repurposing. Although deep learning-based multimodal integration has advanced DTI modeling, current approaches remain limited in capturing complex cross-modal nonlinear dependencies and in reconciling distributional heterogeneity across data modalities. To address these limitations, we propose a multimodal DTI prediction framework termed Asymmetric Dynamic Gated Modulation and Adaptive Latent Signal Fusion (AD-LSF), which is built upon asymmetric dynamic gating modulation and adaptive latent signal fusi
papers.summary: This paper presents AD-LSF, a new deep learning framework that improves drug-target interaction prediction by better handling complex relationships between different data types (like drug chemical structures and protein sequences). The method uses asymmetric dynamic gating and adaptive fusion to overcome limitations of existing multimodal approaches, potentially aiding drug discovery and repurposing efforts.

papers.title: A comprehensive dataset of 32 million pentapeptide structures for high-throughput virtual screening.
papers.abstract: Small peptides are widely used as binders, modulators, and structural motifs, but their conformational flexibility complicates structure-based analysis and high-throughput screening. We present an open dataset of three-dimensional structures for the complete space of canonical amino-acid pentapeptides: 3,200,000 unique sequences with up to 10 conformers per sequence, for a total of 32,000,000 peptide conformers. Structures were generated directly from sequence using an automated workflow built on UCSF ChimeraX for model construction, Reduce for hydrogen placement, and RDKit for conformer gener
papers.summary: Researchers generated an open dataset of 32 million 3D structures covering all possible pentapeptide sequences, providing a resource for high-throughput virtual screening of peptide binders and modulators. The automated workflow using ChimeraX, Reduce, and RDKit enables rapid structure generation from sequence alone.

papers.title: Discovery of potent low-toxicity antimicrobial peptides through diffusion modeling.
papers.abstract: The rapid emergence of multidrug-resistant bacteria has created an urgent need for improved antimicrobial discovery and screening platforms. Here, we present ARCADIAMP, a generative and virtual screening platform that couples an iterative-learning discrete denoising diffusion probabilistic model with a two-stage Evolutionary Scale Modeling 2 (ESM2)-based antibacterial activity classifier to generate, classify, and prioritize potent AMPs with high activity, low toxicity, and favorable serum stability. Eight of the ten experimentally screened peptide candidates showed antimicrobial activity (MIC
papers.summary: Researchers developed ARCADIAMP, a platform combining a diffusion model with an AI classifier to design new antimicrobial peptides. The system successfully generated peptides with strong activity against bacteria and low toxicity, with 8 out of 10 tested candidates showing antimicrobial effects.

papers.title: ScrambleBench: a workflow for comparative assessment of structure-based de novo generative models.
papers.abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (AI) has rapidly advanced over the past decade in the field of drug discovery, particularly for the de novo design of small molecules based on target protein structures. While generative AI has the potential to complement traditional structure-based drug design (SBDD) to discover novel hits, their performance is often assessed using non-standardized evaluation criteria. As the number of generative AI models continue to grow, it becomes increasingly important to determine whether these tools are sufficiently robust and reliable for integration into medicinal c
papers.summary: This paper introduces ScrambleBench, a standardized workflow for comparing structure-based generative AI models used in drug discovery. The authors address the problem of non-standardized evaluation criteria by providing a consistent framework to assess the robustness and reliability of these models for de novo small molecule design.

papers.title: Smiles-based bioactivity prediction through molecular encoder selection and data augmentation.
papers.abstract: Quantitative prediction of inhibitor potency can accelerate early-stage drug discovery. Recently, data-driven approaches have gained widespread interest in drug discovery, as evidenced by a growing number of benchmarking challenges and open competitions. In this context, we developed a machine learning-based methodology that can find the most effective way of predicting IC50 values against ASK1 from SMILES, for "Jump AI(.py) 2025: 3rd AI Drug Discovery Competition", hosted by the Korea Pharmaceutical and Bio-Pharma Manufacturers Association (KPBMA) on the Dacon platform. Applying our methodolo
papers.summary: This study presents a machine learning approach for predicting inhibitor potency (IC50 values) against ASK1 from SMILES strings, developed for a drug discovery competition. The methodology focuses on selecting optimal molecular encoders and using data augmentation to improve prediction accuracy. This work demonstrates a practical pipeline for early-stage drug discovery using SMILES-based bioactivity prediction.

papers.title: A multi-fidelity tabular prior-data fitted network model for accurate prediction and uncertainty quantification.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of unknown labels from feature-label datasets using machine learning is critical for applications spanning drug discovery, disease diagnostics, and climate science. However, challenges persist with limited data, high-dimensional inputs, and multi-fidelity scenarios. We developed multi-fidelity tabular prior-data fitted network (MFTabPFN), a general-purpose multi-fidelity model integrating low- and high-fidelity data through a hierarchical transformer architecture to enhance prediction accuracy and uncertainty quantification (UQ). MFTabPFN captures cross-fidelity correlation
papers.summary: This paper introduces MFTabPFN, a machine learning model that integrates low- and high-quality data to improve predictions and quantify uncertainty, especially when data is limited or high-dimensional. The model uses a hierarchical transformer architecture to learn from both data sources, enhancing accuracy in applications like drug discovery and diagnostics.

papers.title: SMASH: Screening Molecules Accurately on Small Hardware. Fast, user-friendly, enhanced with a machine learning virtual screening tool.
papers.abstract: In 2025, we released UAM-Ixachi to democratize, simplify, and accelerate molecular docking and virtual screening methods. It is a free, open-source, and user-friendly tool. Building on that work, we present SMASH, which features several upgrades: it predicts binding sites using machine learning, automatically determines titration states, utilizes a Graphics Process Unit for molecular docking, combines machine learning with energy scoring functions, and clusters data using the K-means algorithm. Tests demonstrated that the tool might handle large projects within a reasonable time using local co
papers.summary: SMASH is an upgraded, free, and open-source virtual screening tool that uses machine learning to predict binding sites and combines it with energy scoring functions to accelerate molecular docking on standard hardware. It automatically handles titration states and uses GPU acceleration, making large-scale drug screening projects more accessible and efficient for researchers.

papers.title: Targeting HsDHODH: Shape and machine-learning guided discovery and structural validation of SARS-CoV-2 antivirals.
papers.abstract: The frequent emergence of novel RNA viruses and the rapid development of viral resistance highlight the urgent need for new antiviral therapies. In this study, we employed an integrated computational pipeline combining shape-based models, machine-learning (ML)-based models, and hotspot analysis to identify novel human dihydroorotate dehydrogenase (HsDHODH) inhibitors. A virtual screening (VS) campaign of the H3D chemical library identified H3D-002856 as the primary hit (IC50 = 2.92 ± 0.07 μM), which subsequently guided a similarity-based hit expansion (Tanimoto coefficient≥0.7) of 17 structura
papers.summary: Researchers used a combination of shape-based modeling, machine learning, and hotspot analysis to discover new inhibitors of human dihydroorotate dehydrogenase (HsDHODH) as potential antivirals against SARS-CoV-2. The virtual screening campaign identified H3D-002856 as a primary hit with an IC50 of 2.92 μM, and similarity-based expansion yielded 17 additional structural analogs. This integrated computational approach offers a promising strategy for rapid antiviral drug discovery.

papers.title: P2MAT: A machine learning (ML) driven software for Property Prediction of MATerial.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of melting points for pure molecules remains a significant challenge in predictive chemistry, with implications across various scientific fields, including materials science, drug discovery, and separations chemistry. Traditional methods, such as group contribution (GC) techniques, have shown limited success due to the complex relationship between molecular structure and melting point. In this study, we present a data-driven machine learning (ML) approach to predict the melting points of organic compounds, leveraging both 2D and 3D molecular descriptors. Our results indicat
papers.summary: This paper introduces P2MAT, a machine learning software that predicts melting points of organic compounds using 2D and 3D molecular descriptors, outperforming traditional group contribution methods. The tool addresses a long-standing challenge in predictive chemistry with potential applications in materials science and drug discovery.

papers.title: Efficient and valid large molecule generation via self-supervised generative models.
papers.abstract: The realm of molecular design, particularly for large molecules, presents unique challenges and opportunities in drug discovery and materials science. Large molecule design is inherently more complex and less explored compared to designing small molecules, adding significant difficulty in generative modeling. We aim to establish strong baselines for better scalability, efficiency, and generative performance in this domain. We evaluate the scalability and performance of generative AI models, initially effective for small molecule design, in generating large molecules for potential drugs in gene
papers.summary: This paper establishes strong baselines for generating large molecules using self-supervised generative models, addressing the unique challenges of complexity and scalability in this underexplored domain. The authors evaluate AI models originally designed for small molecules, demonstrating their potential for efficient and valid large molecule drug design.

papers.title: Generative AI for controllable protein sequence design: A survey.
papers.abstract: The design of novel protein sequences with targeted functionalities underpins a central theme in protein engineering, impacting diverse fields such as drug discovery and enzymatic engineering. However, navigating this vast combinatorial search space remains a severe challenge due to time and financial constraints. This scenario is rapidly evolving as the transformative advancements in AI have been propelling the protein design field into a new era. In this survey, we systematically review recent advances in generative AI for controllable protein sequence design. To set the stage, we first outl
papers.summary: This survey reviews recent advances in using generative AI to design novel protein sequences with targeted functions, which is crucial for drug discovery and enzyme engineering. It highlights how AI is transforming the field by enabling navigation of the vast protein sequence space more efficiently than traditional methods.

papers.title: Commentary on the fundamentals and development of artificial intelligence models in the life sciences and best research practices.
papers.abstract: Machine learning has a long history in the life and natural sciences. In the age of artificial intelligence (AI), both the opportunities and challenges associated with predictive and generative modeling are increasing. To exert significant influence on experimental programs in interdisciplinary research, AI models and their predictions must be demystified as far as possible, and misconceptions regarding what highly complex models can and cannot do must be avoided. Furthermore, it is necessary to evaluate best practices for the use of AI in life science research including drug discovery. This c
papers.summary: This commentary discusses the opportunities and challenges of using AI models in life sciences, emphasizing the need to demystify complex models and avoid misconceptions about their capabilities. It highlights best practices for AI in interdisciplinary research, including drug discovery, to ensure meaningful impact on experimental programs.

papers.title: Computational design of low-volatility lubricants for space using interpretable machine learning.
papers.abstract: The function and lifetime of moving mechanical assemblies (MMAs) in space depend on the properties of lubricants. MMAs that experience high speeds or high cycles require liquid-based lubricants due to their ability to reflow to the point of contact. However, only a few liquid-based lubricants have vapor pressures low enough for the vacuum conditions of space, each of which has limitations that add constraints to MMA designs. This work introduces a data-driven machine learning (ML) approach to predicting vapor pressure, enabling virtual screening and discovery of new space-suitable liquid lubri
papers.summary: This study uses machine learning to predict vapor pressure of liquid lubricants, enabling virtual screening for compounds stable in space vacuum conditions. The approach addresses the scarcity of suitable lubricants for moving mechanical assemblies in spacecraft by computationally identifying new candidates.

papers.title: 3DICE: Interpretable 3D Cross-Modal Learning for Drug-Target Interaction Prediction and Large-Scale Drug Discovery.
papers.abstract: Drug-target interaction (DTI) prediction is a crucial step in modern drug discovery. Accurate and efficient predictions can substantially reduce costs and development time. Applications of deep learning methods for this purpose have been extensively studied in recent years, yielding instrumental contributions to this field. However, existing methods face issues pertaining to efficient learning of drug and target feature representations, which is detrimental to generalisability and performance in cold-start scenarios. Most approaches extract representations from SMILES strings for drugs and FAS
papers.summary: This paper presents 3DICE, a deep learning method that uses 3D structural information from both drugs and targets to improve drug-target interaction prediction. The model achieves better performance in cold-start scenarios, where it must predict interactions for unseen drugs or targets, and can be applied to large-scale drug discovery.

papers.title: Bridging the phenotype-target gap for molecular generation via multi-objective reinforcement learning.
papers.abstract: The generation of high-quality candidate molecules remains a central challenge in AI-driven drug design. Current phenotype-based and target-based strategies each suffer limitations, either incurring high experimental costs or overlooking system-level cellular responses. To bridge this gap, we propose XMolRL, a novel generative framework that synergistically integrates phenotypic and target-specific cues for de novo molecular generation.
papers.summary: XMolRL is a new AI framework that combines information about cellular effects (phenotype) and specific protein targets to generate better drug candidate molecules. It uses multi-objective reinforcement learning to balance these two types of information, overcoming limitations of current approaches that focus on only one aspect.

papers.title: Agentomics: an agentic system that autonomously develops novel state-of-the-art solutions for biomedical machine learning tasks.
papers.abstract: Extracting knowledge from biomedical data is crucial for advancing our understanding of biological systems and developing novel therapeutics. The quantity, quality, and resolution of biomedical data constantly evolves, requiring the automation of biomedical machine learning (ML). Existing Automated ML tools lack flexibility, while large language models (LLMs) struggle to consistently deliver reproducible machine learning codebases, and existing LLM Agent-powered solutions lag behind human-engineered ML models.
papers.summary: Agentomics is a new AI agent system that can autonomously design and implement machine learning solutions for biomedical problems, outperforming both existing automated ML tools and human-engineered models. It combines the flexibility of large language models with robust code generation to produce reproducible, state-of-the-art results across diverse biomedical tasks.

papers.title: Application of hydrogels in 3D tumor microenvironment modeling and drug screening for cervical cancer: A review.
papers.abstract: Cervical cancer, driven mainly by human papillomavirus (HPV) infection, remains one of the most common malignant tumors among women worldwide, posing significant challenges in treatment and drug development. Traditional two-dimensional (2D) cell culture models fail to accurately replicate the in vivo tumor microenvironment (TME), especially HPV-driven oncogenic signaling, immune contexture, and stromal interactions unique to cervical cancer, limiting their predictive value for therapeutic efficacy (Y. Liu, H. Ai. Comprehensive insights into human papillomavirus and cervical cancer: pathophysio
papers.summary: This review discusses how hydrogels can be used to create 3D tumor microenvironment models for cervical cancer, which better replicate in vivo conditions than traditional 2D cultures. The authors highlight that these 3D models improve drug screening accuracy by capturing HPV-driven signaling, immune interactions, and stromal components unique to cervical cancer.

papers.title: GraphTransDTI: A novel hybrid framework combining graph transformer and CNN-BiLSTM for enhanced Drug-Protein Interaction prediction.
papers.abstract: Drug-protein interaction (DTI) prediction is a pivotal step in the drug discovery and repurposing process, helping to minimize experimental costs and time. However, existing deep learning methods often face limitations in simultaneously capturing the spatial structure of drug molecules and the deep contextual correlation with protein sequences. To address this issue, we propose GraphTransDTI, a synergistic hybrid framework that integrates a Graph Transformer to represent drug graph structures, a CNN-BiLSTM network to encode protein sequence context, and a Cross-Attention mechanism to model cro
papers.summary: GraphTransDTI is a new deep learning framework that combines a Graph Transformer for drug molecular structures with a CNN-BiLSTM network for protein sequences, using cross-attention to model their interactions. It improves drug-protein interaction prediction, which could accelerate drug discovery and repurposing by reducing experimental costs.

papers.title: AI-driven drug reposition for pathogens: a new paradigm in pandemic preparedness.
papers.abstract: Faced with a severe outbreak of diseases caused by newly emerging and recurrent pathogens, the development cycle of traditional drugs is long, making it difficult to meet emergency needs. Drug repositioning has become a key strategy for rapidly providing therapies by exploring new therapeutic uses of approved drugs. However, traditional reposition methods have bottlenecks such as slow speed and strong randomness. Artificial intelligence (AI) is revolutionizing drug reposition by analyzing and integrating multi-source data with computational models, dramatically accelerating the discovery proce
papers.summary: This review discusses how AI-driven drug repositioning can accelerate pandemic preparedness by rapidly identifying new uses for existing drugs against emerging pathogens, overcoming the slow speed and randomness of traditional methods. The authors argue that AI's ability to integrate multi-source data makes it a key strategy for providing therapies faster than conventional drug development.

papers.title: Computer-aided drug discovery: historical foundations, practical AI tools, and emerging ethical considerations.
papers.abstract: Computer-aided drug discovery (CADD) has become an integral component of modern drug development, supporting hit identification, lead optimisation, and candidate refinement across both academia and industry. Over 4 decades of methodological progress have contributed to the discovery or optimisation of several approved therapeutics, establishing CADD as a crucial element of drug research. In parallel, recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) have introduced a new generation of practical tools that offer improved predictive performance, accessible software implem
papers.summary: This review covers the historical evolution of computer-aided drug discovery (CADD) over four decades, highlighting its role in approved therapeutics. It then surveys recent AI/ML tools that improve predictive performance and accessibility, while also discussing emerging ethical considerations in their application.

papers.title: From genomes to interventions: computational strategies transforming parasitology.
papers.abstract: In silico techniques have become essential in contemporary parasitology, offering swift, cost-effective, and scalable methods for exploring parasite biology, host-parasite interactions, drug resistance, the discovery of diagnostic markers, vaccine development, and the prioritization of therapeutic targets. Computational frameworks that encompass genomics, pangenomics, phylogenetics, transcriptomics, proteomics, structural biology, molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulations, artificial intelligence (AI), and immunoinformatics have collectively revolutionized parasite research. They enabl
papers.summary: This review highlights how computational methods—including genomics, AI, and structural biology—are transforming parasitology by enabling faster, cheaper analysis of parasite biology, drug resistance, and vaccine targets. These in silico approaches are now essential for discovering diagnostic markers and prioritizing therapeutic interventions.
